figures.caption: Figure 1. Single-metric ansatz search. (a) Expressibility vs number of parameters (|θ|). (b) Trainability vs number of gates (G). (c) Entanglement vs circuit depth (D). (d) Expressibility vs search space complexity. Markers denote quantum circuit ansätze (see legend), comparing the circuit found by the ansatz search (A) to the benchmark circuits ir from [12], where r is the number of repetitions. Where applicable, the horizontal band labeled Haar indicates a ±3σ interval estimated from 5,000 (pair) Haar-random states for the corresponding metric. Marker size encodes a proxy for circuit complexity: in (a) ∝ √ G, in (b) ∝ √ D (depth), and in (c) ∝ √ |θ|. Panel (d) shows the hardware-constrained expressibility search on the 5-qubit IQM Spark device (Spark ansatz search) using its native gate set and star connectivity.
Figure 18. Circuit diagram of the LiH ansatz returned by the multi-objective expressibility and trainability search (ASexcitation).

Figure 2. Multi-objective ansatz search. Each point corresponds to a circuit ir from the benchmark families of [12] (across repetitions r) and the circuit discovered by our search (A). The horizontal band labeled Haar indicates a ±3σ interval estimated from 5,000 pair Haar-random states for the expressibility metric. (a) Shows the expressibility vs. trainability and (b) expressibility vs. entanglement. For visualization purposes, we set the expressibility threshold to 0.003, the trainability threshold to 2.5, and the entanglement threshold to 0.85.

Table IV. Detailed multi-objective results. The results are shown for the benchmark circuits ir from [12] and the ansätze found by our search (ASExpr−Ent, ASExpr−Train). For each circuit ID and repetition r ∈ {1, . . . , 5} we report parameter count |θ|, gate count G, depth D, and the achieved metric values (expressibility, entanglement, trainability). Entries marked with “–” indicate metrics that are not applicable to the corresponding multi-objective run.

Table I. Search-space constraints and metric thresholds for all experiments. For each experiment, the table lists the number of qubits (n), the allowed gate set (G), the connectivity/topology constraint (T ) when applicable, the bounds imposed on parameter count (|θ|), gate count (Gmax), and circuit depth (Dmax) during ansatz search, as well as the corresponding number of optimization trials (Ntrials), metric thresholds used in the objective (τExpr, τBP, τEnt), and the epsilon truncation (εExpr).

Figure 6. Sensitivity of metric estimates to the number of sampled initial states in the QNN setting. For all baseline circuits and the reference QNN of [5], metric values are computed by averaging over |S0| = Mk randomly drawn (feature-map encoded) input states with (Mk) = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10, 15, 20) and over circuit parameters uniformly sampled i.i.d. from [−1, 1]|θ| following [5]. (a) Stepwise change in expressibility E between consecutive sample sizes, ∆Ek := E(Mk)−E(Mk−1). (b) Stepwise change in trainability T , ∆Tk := T (Mk)− T (Mk−1).

Figure 3. QNN case study following the setup of [5]. (a) Search outcomes in the expressibility–trainability plane for the considered search space. (b) Estimated effective dimension for the reference QNN architecture from [5] and for the discovered PQC. (c) Training performance on the dataset of [5], using the same optimizer and protocol, comparing the reference architecture to our discovered PQC. For visualization purposes, we set the expressibility and trainability thresholds to 0.003 and 2.5, respectively.

Table V. Detailed QNN results. The results are shown for the benchmark circuits ir from [12], the QNN from [5], and the ansätze found by our search (AS). For each circuit ID and repetition r ∈ {1, . . . , 5} we report parameter count |θ|, gate count G, depth D, and the achieved metric values (expressibility, trainability). Entries marked with “–” indicate metrics that are not applicable to the corresponding ansatz search run.

Table III. Expressibility ansatz search on quantum hardware. The expressibility and effective circuit complexity are shown after compiling the benchmark circuits from [12] and the discovered ansätze to the IQM Spark native basis and star connectivity. We report |θ|, gate count G, depth D, and expressibility across repetitions r ∈ {1, . . . , 5}. Gate count and depth are reported after transpilation to the native gate set {CZ,RZ} and star topology. The last rows summarize the hardware-constrained ansatzsearch result (ASExpr) and the best circuit obtained from the 10,000-trial simulation in the same restricted gate set/topology (ASExpr,10k).

Figure 5. (a) Bloch-sphere intuition for the single-qubit family generated by Rz(θ) under two inputs: starting from |0⟩, the state remains fixed on the Bloch sphere (localized ensemble), whereas starting from |+⟩ the state traces the equator as θ varies (spread ensemble). (b) Binned pairwise-fidelity histograms for the corresponding n-qubit product ensemble produced by applying Rz(θi) on each qubit i starting from |+⟩ (with 75 bins), compared against the discretized Haar reference as n increases.

Figure 4. VQE evaluation of ansätze discovered by metric-driven search. (a) Metric values of the discovered ansatz and reference circuits for H2 at R = 0.75 Å, including expressibility, trainability, and circuit complexity. (b) VQE energy convergence for H2 across multiple interatomic distances using a single fixed ansatz optimized at R = 0.75 Å, compared against UCCSD and hardware-efficient baselines. (c) Proof-of-concept LiH VQE at R = 1.5 Å (100 Bayesian-optimization iterations). For visualization purposes, we set the expressibility and trainability thresholds to 0.003 and 2.5, respectively.

Table VI. Detailed H2 results. The results are shown for the benchmark circuits ir from [12] including circuit 10 as HEA, the HEA SU2 from Figure 7, the UCCSD [2], and the ansätze found by our search (AS). For each circuit ID and repetition r ∈ {1, . . . , 5} we report parameter count |θ|, gate count G, depthD, and the achieved metric values (expressibility, trainability). Gate count and depth are reported after transpilation to the native gate set {CZ,RZ}. Entries marked with “–” indicate metrics that are not applicable to the corresponding ansatz search run.

Table II. Detailed single-objective results. The results are shown for the benchmark circuits ir from [12] and the ansätze found by our search (ASExpr, ASEnt, ASTrain). For each circuit ID and repetition r ∈ {1, . . . , 5} we report parameter count |θ|, gate count G, depth D, and the achieved metric values (expressibility, entanglement, trainability). Entries marked with “–” indicate metrics that are not applicable to the corresponding single-objective run.
